RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 7,8-dimethyl-10-(2,3,4,5-tetrahydroxypentyl)benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also known as vitamin B2 and also referred to as Riboflavin, is an important water-soluble vitamin whose structure consists of an isoalloxazine aromatic ring and a ribitol side chain, adopting a flat yet slightly flexible molecular configuration; it serves as a precursor for flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), two molecules that act as coenzymes in various redox reactions in organisms, playing a crucial role in cellular energy metabolism and biosynthetic processes, and as a weak competitive ligand, riboflavin can enter the aptamer pocket of bacterial FMN riboswitches, and although its affinity is lower than that of FMN, it can still induce conformational changes in RNA. In the article, the binding ability of riboflavin to the FMN riboswitch was studied, revealing its role in regulating gene expression: riboflavin can bind to the FMN riboswitch, thereby regulating the expression of genes related to riboflavin biosynthesis and transport, and partially inhibiting the expression of downstream riboflavin synthesis genes, thereby participating in the negative feedback regulation of its own biosynthesis; when riboflavin or its derivative FMN binds to the riboswitch, it induces the formation of a transcription termination structure, thereby inhibiting the expression of downstream genes, a mechanism that is vital for maintaining the homeostasis of riboflavin levels in cells.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-4-oxo-1-[3-(pyridin-2-ylamino)propyl]-7-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)quinoline-3-carboxylicacid This is a quinoline-3-carboxylic acid derivative featuring a 4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazine substituent at position 7, which lends the molecule considerable basicity and steric bulk. Its substituent at the N1 position is a 3-(pyridin-2-ylamino)propyl chain. Compared to the double-piperazine analogues, this structure employs a simpler secondary amine-linked pyridine ring at N1. This structural simplification is intended to optimize the molecules binding geometry within the target binding pocket and potentially alter its metabolic rate. Based on its quinolone core, this compound may be researched for antimicrobial or anti-cancer applications, with the side-chain design tailored to explore its selectivity for specific receptors or enzymes.

Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-7-[4-(1,3-benzothiazol-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-4-oxo-1-(2-pyridin-2-ylethyl)-1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylicacid This is a structurally complex quinolone analogue, centered on a 1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylic acid backbone, which is a common core for quinolone antibiotics. The molecule is substituted at position 7 with a complex amine: 4-(1,3-benzothiazol-2-yl)piperazine. The piperazine ring is common in drugs, increasing solubility and basicity, and benzothiazole is another important heterocycle. At the N1 position, it is substituted with a 2-(pyridin-2-yl)ethyl side chain. This intricate substitution pattern, particularly the combination of the naphthyridinone core with the piperazine, pyridine, and carboxylic acid functionalities, strongly suggests this compound is being investigated as a novel broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, primarily through the inhibition of bacterial DNA gyrase or topoisomerase IV.

RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Theophylline, a 1,3-dimethylxanthine structurally related to caffeine (differing from caffeine only by lacking a methyl group at the N7 position), is a naturally occurring alkaloid in green and black tea that acts as a bronchodilator, vasodilator, muscle relaxant, adenosine-receptor antagonist and phosphodiesterase inhibitor; as a ligand, it binds theophylline-binding RNA in a Mg-dependent way, via hydrogen bonds with the RNAs C22 and U24, supported by base triples, and RNA discriminates it from caffeine via such structural interactions. Despite its narrow therapeutic index, the SELEX-derived 38-nt RNA aptamer TCT8-4 binds theophylline with high affinity and high specificity over caffeine, exploiting a hydrogen bond to N-7, favorable contacts with the N-3 methyl and C-2/C-6 carbonyls, and a steric barrier against C-8 substitution, thereby providing a high-resolution RNA sensor for this multifunctional drug and human serum metabolite.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: phenazin-1-ol, also referred to as 1-hydroxyPhenazine, is a hydroxyl derivative of the phenazine scaffold, and this compound is structurally an azaaromatic compound with a phenazine core (two fused benzene rings flanking two nitrogen atoms) and a hydroxyl group at the 1-position, possessing planar and hydrophobic properties typical of azaaromatics, phenazine, a heterocycle fused from two benzene rings and two nitrogen atoms, is known for its strong redox activity, and many phenazine derivatives are natural pigments and antibiotics produced by microorganisms (like Pseudomonas aeruginosa), the introduction of 1-hydroxy group gives the molecule phenolic chemical properties and strong redox couple characteristics, in reality, phenazine derivatives and their analogues are generally investigated as antimicrobial agents, as they can act by interfering with microbial electron transport chains or by generating reactive oxygen species, additionally, it may find applications in electrochemical analysis and dye chemistry as a redox indicator or a dye precursor, functionally, though the document focuses on azaaromatics binding yjdF riboswitches, no information on its riboswitch binding affinity or gene expression effects is given. 1-RNA riboswitch

Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as cIMP, is a highly stereospecific compound whose chemical name indicates a purine base (1H-purin-6-one, a tautomer of hypoxanthine or guanine) linked to a complex sugar analogue; structurally, cIMP has an inosine base, a ribose ring, and a 3,5-cyclic monophosphate ester linkage, typical of cyclic nucleotides, while the attached cyclic structure of this compound is a fused furan/dioxaphosphinane system bearing a phosphonate group, containing multiple chiral centers (4aR,6R,7R,7aS). This structure mimics the form of natural nucleosides or nucleotides, which are cornerstones of energy metabolism, signaling, and genetic material replication in biological systems; functionally, as a cyclic nucleotide analog, it may potentially regulate cyclic nucleotide-mediated signaling (e.g., via interacting with related enzymes/ion channels), but no roles like riboswitch binding or bacterial gene regulation are mentioned in the document. Consequently, nucleoside analogues like 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-1H-purin-6-one (also referred to as cIMP) are typically designed to interfere with intracellular nucleic acid synthesis or function. Its potential real-world activities include use as an antiviral drug (by inhibiting viral reverse transcriptase or polymerase to block viral replication) or an anticancer agent (by being incorporated into DNA/RNA chains or inhibiting key enzymes to disrupt cell proliferation). The stereochemical structure is paramount for its recognition by biological targets and is a critical factor in drug design.

Small_molecule_information: 1H-pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as lumazine, is a keto derivative of the Pteridine scaffold, with the formula C6H4N4O2. Its structure is formed by the fusion of a pyrimidine ring and a pyrazine ring (a bicyclic pteridine core, a fused pyrimidine-pyrazine ring system) containing multiple nitrogen atoms, with two keto groups at positions 2 and 4, giving it a planar, hydrophobic structure, and it is a flavin precursor/derivative that binds to yjdF (azaaromatic) riboswitches, inducing conserved RNA conformational changes, though unlike high-affinity ligands (e.g., chelerythrine), it does not activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression, nor does it act as the riboswitchs natural ligand, which remains unidentified; the pteridine backbone is of fundamental biological significance as it forms the core of many important biological pigments and cofactors, for instance, it constitutes the base structure of molecules such as Folic acid (Vitamin B9) and Biopterin, which are essential coenzymes for crucial metabolic processes in the body, including one-carbon unit transfer, amino acid metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis, and as a foundational structure, its derivatives hold significant importance in nutrition, drug development (particularly for microbial metabolism and cancer therapy), and biochemical research.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoic acid, also referred to as pteroic acid, is a non-azaaromatic folate precursor that is the hydrolytic product of folic acid lacking the glutamate residue, structured with an oxidized pteridine ring covalently linked to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA)lacking the glutamic acid moiety that defines folic acid and also lacking planar azaaromatic features, this molecule is an important intermediate in the folate biosynthetic pathway of certain bacteria, where organisms like bacteria synthesize it first then add glutamic acid to form folic acid while humans cannot make it and require full folate from the diet, and it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; the compound itself lacks direct therapeutic use, but it is critical in the synthesis of folate analogs and inhibitors, for instance, sulfonamide drugs are structurally similar to PABA and exert their antibacterial effect by inhibiting bacterial folate synthesis.

Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(2-amino-6-oxo-1H-purin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Guanosine 5-monophosphate (5-GMP or GMP), is a non-azaaromatic nucleotide structured with a guanine base, a ribose sugar, and a single phosphate group attached to the riboses 5-carbon (i.e., guanosine phosphorylated at the C5 position of the ribose), which lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; GMP is a necessary precursor for DNA and RNA synthesis, a crucial intracellular metabolic intermediate and signaling molecule as well as a building block for RNA and a precursor for higher guanine nucleotides (e.g., GDP, GTP), in practical applications, GMP and its salts (like disodium guanylate) are extensively used as a food additive, functioning as a flavor enhancer (known as an umami agent) to boost the savory taste of foods, and in biomedical research, GMP is a fundamental reagent for studying purine metabolism, cell signaling, and enzymology.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[4-(3-amino-1-oxo-2,5,6,6a,7,9-hexahydroimidazo[1,5-f]pteridin-8-yl)benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as 5,10-Methylene tetrahydrofolate (5,10-methylene THF), is a folic acid analog featuring a highly modified pteridine system, specifically a hexahydroimidazo[1,5-f]pteridine core linked to a glutamate residue via a PABA segment, while 5,10-methylene THF itself is a non-azaaromatic folate derivative with a methylene group (-CH-) linking the 5th and 10th positions of the tetrahydrofolate (THF) pteridine ring that lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis and serves as a core one-carbon carrier in metabolism transferring methylene units for thymidine synthesis and homocysteine remethylation which is critical for nucleic acid production and amino acid metabolism; structural modifications of the folate skeleton, especially those incorporating fused ring systems, are typically designed to create novel antifolate agents, the activity of such compounds is focused on the inhibition of key enzymes in the folate metabolic pathway, such as dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) or thymidylate synthase (TS), these enzymes are essential for nucleotide biosynthesis in cancer cells and various pathogens, therefore, the molecule is primarily researched as a potential novel anticancer or antimicrobial drug. 5,10-methylene THF5,10-methenyl THF

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-7,8-dihydro-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as Dihydrofolic acid (DHF), is commonly known as Folic acid, or Vitamin B9, with the molecular formula C19H19N7O6, and Dihydrofolic acid (DHF) is a folate derivative with a structure comprising a pteridine ring, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), and glutamate moiety, lacking one reduced hydrogen pair vs. tetrahydrofolate (THF), its structure comprises a pteridine ring, a p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) residue, and a glutamic acid residue, its pteridine core enables one-carbon transfer reactions, in terms of real-world activity, Folic acid is an essential water-soluble vitamin for humans and many organisms, it is reduced in vivo to the active form, Tetrahydrofolate (THF), which serves as a one-carbon unit carrier, participating in vital biochemical reactions, including the synthesis of nucleic acid precursors like purines (A and G) and thymidine (T), as well as amino acid metabolism, functionally, DHF acts as a key intermediate in folate metabolism, converted to THF by dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), it supports nucleotide biosynthesis and amino acid metabolism, clinically, Folic acid is used for the treatment of megaloblastic anemia and is crucially administered during early pregnancy to prevent fetal neural tube defects, the folic acid metabolic pathway is also a key target for many anticancer drugs (e.g., Methotrexate) and antimicrobial agents, and its analogs are used as anticancer/antibacterial agents by inhibiting DHFR to disrupt cellular proliferation.

Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-2-hydroxybenzoic acid, also referred to as 4-Aminosalicylic acid or p-Aminosalicylic acid (PAS), is a salicylate drug and salicylic acid derivative characterized by an amino group at position 4, a hydroxyl group, and a carboxyl group attached to a benzene ring, with the molecular formula C7H7NO3; it has a strong binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA but does not induce gene expression, suggesting that it may not trigger relevant biological function, and in terms of real-world activity, it is a historically significant antituberculosis drug that played a pivotal role in the early treatment of the disease, currently still used as a second-line agent for treating multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB), its mechanism of action involves acting as a competitive inhibitor of p-Aminobenzoic acid (PABA), thereby disrupting the function of dihydropteroate synthase in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, which ultimately blocks the synthesis of essential folic acid and inhibits bacterial growth, and due to its salicylic acid structure, it also possesses some anti-inflammatory properties.

Small_molecule_information: (4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-6-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-2-hydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-7-ol, also referred to as adenosine 3,5-cyclic monophosphate (cAMP), is a complex adenine nucleoside analog that is a non-azaaromatic cyclic nucleotide with an adenosine core (adenine ribose) and a single phosphate group forming a 3,5 cyclic bond, which lacks planar azaaromatic features and binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; its core structure is adenine (6-aminopurin-9-yl) linked to a highly modified and complex sugar ring, which features a fused cyclic phosphate structure (furo[3,2d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin), with the molecular formula C10H12N5O7P and multiple chiral centers, and in terms of real-world activity, this compound, due to its cyclic nucleotide structural feature, is considered a synthetic second messenger analog, mimicking molecules like cAMP or cGMP, may be designed as an enzyme substrate or inhibitor to disrupt intracellular signaling pathways, and given the importance of nucleoside analogs in antiviral and anticancer drugs, this molecule may exhibit potential in regulating cell proliferation or immune responses by competing with ATP or acting as a non-hydrolyzable cAMP analog, affecting key metabolic enzymes like adenylate cyclases and phosphodiesterases, while its core role is also acting as a second messenger in cellular signaling, regulating processes like metabolism and gene expression, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing.

Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methylquinoline is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core, with a methyl substituent at the 7-position on the benzene portion. The molecular formula is C10H9N. The compound exhibits a planar conjugated system. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4S)-5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin mononucleotide (FMN), is a phosphate-containing pteridine derivative and a nucleotide-like coenzyme and flavin compound that binds to yjdF motif RNAs (azaaromatic riboswitches), inducing structural changes in the RNA as shown by in-line probing, whose riboflavin moiety is a planar nitrogen-containing polycyclic compound and unlike some azaaromatics fails to trigger riboswitch-mediated gene expression, thus not considered the natural ligand of these riboswitches, its structure is based on the 7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core (an isomer of the flavin or riboflavin core): a tetracyclic fused benzopteridine skeleton, with a phosphated tetrahydroxypentyl side chain attached at the N10-position, the molecular formula is CHNOP, the compound possesses a conjugated skeleton, and the presence of the phosphate group makes it negatively charged and water-soluble under physiological conditions, in terms of biological function, its core derivatives are components of crucial flavin coenzymes (such as FMN, FAD) in living systems, acting as key electron carriers in cellular oxidation-reduction reactions.

Small_molecule_information: (3R,4S,5S,6R,7R,9R,11R,12R,13S,14R)-6-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4-(dimethylamino)-3-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-14-ethyl-7,12,13-trihydroxy-4-[(2R,4R,5S,6S)-5-hydroxy-4-methoxy-4,6-dimethyloxan-2-yl]oxy-3,5,7,9,11,13-hexamethyl-oxacyclotetradecane-2,10-dione, also referred to as Erythromycin, is an extremely complex macrolide structure, representing Erythromycin or its derivatives, and Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic with a 14-membered lactone ring core linked to two deoxy sugars (desosamine and cladinose), its structure features a large cyclic lactone scaffold with glycosidic attachments, enabling specific bacterial ribosome binding, macrolides are important clinical antibiotics that inhibit bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosomal subunit, functionally, it inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by targeting the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking peptide bond formation, and exhibits broad activity against Gram-positive bacteria, being widely used to treat respiratory and skin infections while resisting certain drug-resistant strains.

Small_molecule_information: Quinoline, also referred to as an azaaromatic compound with a planar hydrophobic benzo[b]pyridine scaffold that interacts non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA and shows minimal activation of riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, is one of the most important nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocycles formed by the fusion of a benzene ring with a pyridine ring with the molecular formula, and it is a colorless hygroscopic liquid with a strong odor whose skeleton forms the core of many natural products such as cinchona alkaloids and synthetic drugs such as chloroquine and quinolone antibiotics, with quinoline derivatives widely used in the synthesis of dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals and exhibiting diverse biological activities including antibacterial, antimalarial, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer effects.

Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: The 161 yjdF RNA from B. subtilis, part of the azaaromatic riboswitch class, has a four-stem (P1-P4) structure with conserved regions. It binds diverse azaaromatics  via a single site, triggering similar structural changes. Ligand binding activates gene expression by unblocking the ribosome-binding site, aiding potential detox. It doesn’t bind phenolic acids/bile salts well and differs from FMN riboswitches in ligand specificity.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-fluorophenyl)butyl]-7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as 5FDQD (5-(3-(4-fluorophenyl)butyl)-7,8-dimethylpyrido[3,4-b]quinoline-1,3(2H,5H)-dione), is a flavin analog whose core scaffold is benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, a tricyclic heterocyclic system also known as isoalloxazine, which is central to Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) and its coenzymes (FAD and FMN); the molecule is substituted at the 10-position with a long 4-fluorophenylbutyl chain, a structure indicative of a flavin analog or antagonist, and it shares a similar chemical structure with FMN but features enhanced chemical selectivity and stability due to the replacement of the ribose phosphate group with an arylalkyl moiety; flavins are crucial cofactors for Oxidoreductases in biological systems, therefore this compound likely exerts its effect by interfering with cellular redox reactions or enzyme activity, such as acting as a Monoamine Oxidase (MAO) inhibitor or a regulator of other flavin-dependent enzymes, and in vitro it can bind to the FMN riboswitch, trigger its functional activity, and exhibit comparable potency to FMN, showing potent antibacterial activity against Clostridium difficile and, in mouse models, effectively preventing lethal Clostridium difficile infection (CDI) while exerting minimal impact on the gut microbiota; flavin analogs are investigated for antitumor and antiviral applications, with the lipophilic 4-fluorophenylbutyl group aiding cellular uptake, and these properties suggest that 5FDQD or its analogs hold promise as novel antibacterial agents for the treatment of Clostridium difficile infections.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methyl-3H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as 7-Methylxanthine, is one of the core structures of 7-Methylxanthine and an oxopurine alkaloid in which the hydrogen at N-7 of the xanthine ring is replaced by a methyl group, making it an obligatory intermediate in the plant biosynthetic pathway to caffeine; this compound is found as a plant and mammalian metabolite, and its single methyl substitution abolishes the N-7 proton that the TCT8-4 RNA aptamer uses for high-affinity hydrogen bonding, resulting in much weaker binding to the aptamer than theophylline, which demonstrates that an intact N-7 proton is essential for tight recognition by the evolved RNA sensor, and it is also one of the main metabolites of caffeine in humans and is significant in pharmacokinetic and toxicological studies.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]propan-1-one, also referred to as Compound 6, is a compound containing quinazoline and piperazine structures, which is a derivative of compound 1 where the piperazine secondary amine is modified with a propionyl group (R Et); its structure is based on the quinazoline parent skeleton, substituted at C2 with a piperazinyl group, where the piperazine N1 is further substituted with a propionyl group, this structure is related to the Prazosin class of quinazolines, it binds to the influenza RNA promoter and exhibits antiviral activity with no cytotoxicity, resulting in a favorable IC/CC ratio, and in terms of biological function, quinazoline compounds generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as -receptor blockade (antihypertensive), antitumor, and related pharmacological effects.

Small_molecule_information: (6-carbamimidoylnaphthalen-2-yl) 4-(diaminomethylideneamino)benzoate is an amidinated naphthyl ester, containing an amidine group on the naphthalene ring and a guanidine group (which is also an amidine) on the benzoate ester. This structure is a potential protease inhibitor, especially for serine proteases (such as thrombin, trypsin), as the amidine group is a common pharmacophore for protease inhibitors. The molecule may have applications in anticoagulation or anti-inflammatory fields.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-one, also referred to as CP11, is an isocoumarin derivative, and CP11 is a chromanone (dihydrobenzopyranone) derivative with a phenyl ring whose saturated chromone core exhibited a single, modest-affinity binding mode, lacking the desired specificity for the target RNA structure, the isocoumarin scaffold, fused from a benzene ring and a six-membered lactone ring (dihydro-pyranone), is prevalent in natural products, especially fungal and plant metabolites, these naturally occurring isocoumarin derivatives often exhibit diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-ones structure includes a phenyl group at the 3-position of the lactone ring, which increases the molecules lipophilicity and steric complexity, in reality, compounds with this structure serve as basic building blocks for the synthesis of various natural product analogues and pharmaceutical precursors, where researchers explore and optimize their potential pharmacological activity through further chemical modifications (e.g., introducing other functional groups on the phenyl or isocoumarin rings). Its structure is a derivative of flavone (2-phenylchromen-4-one) featuring a chromone (benzopyranone) core with an oxime group and a phenyl ring, where an oxime group (CNOH) is attached at the 3 position, and the oxime bond has a (Z) geometric isomer configuration. In pharmaceutical chemistry research, oxime derivatives of flavonoids are often explored for metal ion chelation or as enzyme inhibitors. This specific molecule and its analogues are studied for their potential antioxidant and antitumor activities, for instance, by inhibiting certain tyrosine kinases or acting as neuroprotective agents. Such oxime-containing molecules are also sometimes used in drug chemistry to improve lipophilicity or alter metabolic stability, though the presence of the oxime in this compound likely contributed to a very high Kd2 value, indicating poor or non-specific binding and disqualifying it as a candidate.

Small_molecule_information: methyl N-(4-chlorophenyl)-2H-quinoline-1-carboximidothioate, also referred to as CP2, is a quinoline-based molecule featuring a thione and imine functionality (which incorporates a quinoline core linked to a thiocarbamate group and a 4-chlorophenyl ring, with the combination of its planar aromatic system and halogen substituent contributing to its affinity for CAG repeat RNA and efficacy in reducing polyQ aggregation in cellular models, marking it as a promising candidate). Quinoline scaffolds are associated with various anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, and this complex structure is a candidate for research as an enzyme inhibitor, possibly targeting microbial enzymes, due to its specialized reactive groups.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(6-carbamimidoyl-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)ethenyl]-3H-benzimidazole-5-carboximidamide, also referred to as BBE, is a compound containing a bis-benzimidazole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups connected by an ethenyl linker, with the formula C18H16N8; its skeleton is rigidly connected by two benzimidazole-amidine groups through a trans-ethylene bridge, forming a conjugated plane that can be inserted between the twisted double helix and the convex ring of RNA, while its double-terminal amidine group electrostably chelates with phosphate oxygen at multiple points, locking the active site into an inactive conformation, thereby blocking the entry of guanosine and achieving non-competitive inhibition of self-splicing. These are known as benzimidazole diamidines, recognized as some of the most potent DNA minor groove binders; they work by deeply inserting into the minor groove and forming multiple hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions, thus strongly inhibiting DNA replication and transcription, and in reality, they hold immense potential as anticancer, antiviral, and antiparasitic agents, and are widely used as high-sensitivity fluorescent probes for DNA.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-fluorophenyl)-3-methylquinoline, also referred to as CP13, is a fluorine-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core, with a 2-fluorophenyl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position. The molecular formula is CHFN. The compound possesses a planar conjugated system, and the fluorine atom alters the molecules electron distribution. Its optimal structure allows for strong -stacking and specific interactions with the C4A5G6 triad, resulting in effective RNA stabilization and powerful neuroprotective effects in cells, establishing it as the most promising lead. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects, and the introduction of a fluorine atom is typically used to enhance the compounds metabolic stability or improve its membrane permeability.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(2,4-dimethylphenyl)quinoline-5-carboxylic acid, also referred to as CP14, is a quinoline-5-carboxylic acid derivative substituted at the 2-position with a 2,4-dimethylphenyl group; CP14 is specifically characterized as a quinoline carboxylic acid derivative with a 3,4-dimethylphenyl group, and its carboxylic acid moiety and dimethyl substitution may have hampered its permeability or binding, resulting in relegating it to a moderate candidate. The combination of the quinoline ring and the carboxylic acid is a common motif in Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) or tyrosine kinase inhibitors. The carboxylic acid group, negatively charged at physiological pH, is crucial for ionic interaction with the targets active site. The 2,4-dimethylphenyl group increases lipophilicity and contributes to hydrophobic interactions. This molecule is a likely candidate for an anti-inflammatory, analgesic, or anti-tumor (kinase inhibition) agent, with its activity driven by the interaction of the planar aromatic ring and the carboxylate group with the enzyme pocket.

Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-naphthalen-1-ylethyl)pyridine, also referred to as CP16, is a coupled aromatic compound containing pyridine and naphthalene moieties, where the naphthalene ring (two fused benzene rings) is linked to the 4-position of the pyridine ring via an ethyl chain, and this flexible alkyl linkage together with the separated aromatic systems is consistent with its general, non-specific interaction with RNA; both the pyridine and naphthalene rings are hydrophobic and planar structures, which help the molecule interact with lipid membranes or hydrophobic pockets, and such structures are often used to design drugs with the ability to interact with membranes or receptors, while the basic nitrogen atom in the pyridine ring can be ionized, influencing its distribution and activity in vivo

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-chlorophenyl)-3-methylquinoline-5-carboxylic acid, also referred to as CP15, is an aromatic carboxylic acid based on the quinoline scaffold, substituted at the 2-position with a 2-chlorophenyl group, at the 3-position with a methyl group, and at the 5-position with a carboxylic acid group; CP15 is a chloroquinoline carboxylic acid with a methyl group, and the presence of both chloro and carboxylate groups led to a high-affinity site, but the compound was not pursued for detailed mechanistic studies. Quinoline carboxylic acids are commonly used as lead structures for anti-inflammatory drugs or antineoplastic agents. The carboxylic acid group is key for ionic interactions with the targets active site, being negatively charged at physiological pH. The 2-chlorophenyl group imposes steric hindrance, restricting rotation and influencing the overall conformation. This molecule is likely a candidate for a tyrosine kinase (TK) inhibitor or an anti-inflammatory agent, relying on its planar aromatic system and the ionic interaction of the carboxylate.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₂ RNA is a repetitive hairpin-forming sequence that adopts a stable secondary structure characterized by regularly spaced U–U mismatches within a C–G base-paired stem. These noncanonical interactions create specific binding pockets that sequester the splicing regulator MBNL1, thereby disrupting its normal function and driving disease phenotypes such as mis-splicing in myotonic dystrophy type 1. The structured repeats also provide discrete molecular recognition elements that can be selectively targeted by small molecules, enabling competitive displacement of MBNL1 and partial restoration of RNA processing functions.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-[4-[N-(3-azidopropyl)carbamimidoyl]phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as AU-azide, is a highly functionalized probe molecule with the molecular formula C24H23N7O, and AU-azide is a derivative of Compound 2 with an azide group for conjugation; it exhibits even higher AU selectivity and is used to build multivalent compounds like dimers, its core is a planar diaryl-furan (biphenyl-furan) system, which provides hydrophobicity and - stacking ability, the molecule contains two key functional groups: carboximidamide and azido, the carboximidamide group (-C(NH)NH2) is typically positively charged at physiological pH, allowing it to interact strongly with negatively charged biomolecules like DNA; thus, amidine derivatives are often used as DNA minor groove binders with anti-tumor or antimicrobial activity, the azido group (-N3) is a prominent bio-orthogonal chemistry functional group, specifically a core component of Click Chemistry, which permits the molecule to be covalently linked to alkyne-containing partners under specific conditions, therefore, this compound is designed as a bifunctional molecular probe for target identification and bio-orthogonal chemical labeling.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₆ oligonucleotide is a key in vitro model used to study the pathogenic CUG repeat expansion. Its repetitive sequence forms a stable stem-loop structure characterized by canonical GC base pairs and non-canonical, dynamic UU mismatches. This unique structural motif is the primary binding site for both the Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein and the designed small molecule ligands. The RNA was synthetically labeled with a TAMRA fluorophore at its 5' end for binding assays. In these assays, ligand binding to the UU mismatches induces a structural change that quenches the TAMRA fluorescence, allowing the direct measurement of binding affinity (K_D), thus establishing r(CUG)₆ as a critical tool for quantifying ligand-RNA interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 12, is an extremely large, bifunctional molecule whose core structure consists of two acridine rings and is a dimeric ligand with an oligoether linker, its binding affinity was close to that of the monomer, it showed a small bivalent effect and no melting temperature data could be determined for this compound; Acridine is a tricyclic planar aromatic system, the parent structure of famous DNA intercalators, the two acridine units are linked by a flexible tetraethylene glycol (PEG-like) spacer, which enhances the molecules water solubility and biological flexibility, each acridine ring also carries a triazine-butane amine side chain, introducing additional basicity and nucleic acid binding capacity, the structure of this compound represents a classic dimerized drug design, aimed at enhancing its affinity for DNA and cytotoxicity through double intercalation or multivalency effects, in practice, this compound is researched as a potent anti-cancer agent, especially against multi-drug resistant (MDR) cell lines, or as a highly specific fluorescent probe.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 11, is an acridine-triazine dimer with acridine substituted at position 4, and the linker chain is a polyethylamino chain containing three ethylamino units (a potent dimeric ligand featuring an oligoamino linker), molecular formula C57H60N20O2; it exhibited strong activity with notable improvements over the monomer and a substantial bivalent effect, though it was not cell-permeable. In terms of real-world activity, its role as a bifunctional DNA binding agent is similar to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[3-[3-[3-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]propylamino]propylamino]propyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, aiming for high cytotoxic activity through DNA intercalation and strong electrostatic attraction. The polyethylamino chain offers a higher charge density, enabling tighter binding to the DNA backbone. This compound is designed to study the effect of linker chain length and chemical nature on DNA binding specificity, topoisomerase inhibitory activity, and intracellular drug transport, serving as a potential anticancer drug candidate.

RNA information: The SMN2 pre-mRNA contains a critical AGGAAG motif within exon 7, which forms a stem-loop structure (TSL1) with the polypyrimidine tract of intron 6. A C-to-T transition causes predominant skipping of exon 7, leading to a truncated, non-functional SMN protein. This RNA serves as the direct binding target for small-molecule modulators like SMN-C2. Upon binding, the RNA undergoes a specific conformational change in the TSL1 loop and bulge, which creates a new surface for recruiting splicing activator proteins FUBP1 and KHSRP, ultimately correcting the splicing defect.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(6,8-dimethylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-[(3S)-4-ethyl-3-methylpiperazin-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMN-C2, is a coumarin derivative fused with imidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine, and SMN-C2 is an orally available small-molecule splicing modifier that selectively binds the AGGAAG motif in SMN2 pre-mRNA with high affinity, coumarin derivatives are known for their fluorescence and anticoagulant activity, it promotes exon 7 inclusion by inducing an RNA conformational change and recruiting splicing activators FUBP1/KHSRP, thereby increasing functional SMN protein levels, in SMA mouse models, it demonstrates potent cellular activity, rescues motor function, prevents neuromuscular pathology, and significantly extends lifespan, and this compound is used as a fluorescence probe or explored for antithrombotic or enzyme inhibitory potential.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-(trifluoromethyl)-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 6, is a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative containing two trifluoromethyl substituents (with a trifluoromethyl substitution at the pyridine rings C-6, modified from initial active analogs) that enhance its lipophilicity; the thienopyridine scaffold is the structural basis for many kinase inhibitors and anti-inflammatory drugs, and this molecule can bind HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNAkey for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection via interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity though it lacks further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, while it may also be developed as an antineoplastic agent by inhibiting specific tyrosine kinases or signaling pathways to block cancer cell proliferation.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N,6-dimethyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 9, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with an N-methylated amide group, modified from initial active analogs targeting HIV-1 RNAs, which binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs and showing moderate affinity while lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and its core is the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, a fused thiophene and pyridine system, substituted at the 3-position with an amino group and at the 6-position with a methyl group, with the 2-position linked via an N-substituted carboxamide to a methyl group and a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl group, the thienopyridine framework being a significant pharmacophore found in antineoplastic and CNS drugs, the trifluoromethyl (CF3) group being highly lipophilic and electron-withdrawing, often introduced to enhance metabolic stability and membrane permeability, and the N-substituted amide being characteristic of many kinase inhibitors and GPCR antagonists, making this molecule a strong candidate for an enzyme-targeting drug, such as a PDE inhibitor or a kinase inhibitor with anti-inflammatory/anti-cancer activity.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 13, is a dimeric ligand with an oligoether linker that is an acridine-triazine dimer structurally similar to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, but with a longer polyethylene glycol (PEG) like linker chain connecting the two acridine units, containing six ethoxy units (PEG6-like), molecular formula C69H78N20O9; it showed only a small bivalent effect in the SPR-based inhibition assay, indicating poor performance, and in terms of real-world activity, its fundamental function is the same as 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, acting as a potent DNA intercalator/cross-linker and a potential antitumor drug, the significantly extended linker chain is a key optimization in the design, as it affects the molecules pharmacokinetic properties (e.g., solubility, half-life) and pharmacodynamic properties, particularly influencing the span and spatial geometry of the two acridine units across the DNA helix, which may lead to different topoisomerase inhibition selectivity and cytotoxic activity, this fine-tuning is aimed at optimizing drug efficacy and reducing side effects.

RNA information: (CCUG)₈ is an RNA oligonucleotide modeling the pathogenic expanded repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 2 (DM2). It forms a stable hairpin structure with internal loops and serves as a target for evaluating ligand selectivity. Some small molecules can bind and cleave (CCUG)₈, while others  show no activity, highlighting its role in distinguishing DM2-specific targeting.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[6-[2-(3-aminophenyl)-3H-benzimidazol-5-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]aniline, also referred to as Compound H1, is a complex bis-benzimidazole compound (a bis-benzimidazole analog derived from Hoechst 33258) with a structure composed of two benzimidazole units and two meta-aminophenyl units linked covalently, molecular formula C26H21N5; the molecule exhibits a polyheterocyclic structure that is highly conjugated and notably planar, featuring multiple amino and imidazole nitrogen atoms capable of extensive hydrogen bonding, and it retains the core DNA/RNA-binding benzimidazole scaffold of Hoechst 33258, which was rationally designed to target the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and optimized through chemical-similarity screening to gain higher affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic 5CUG/3GUC RNA motif; in terms of real-world activity, this bis-benzimidazole structure is highly analogous to the structural characteristics of known DNA minor groove binders (e.g., Hoechst dyes), it is designed for high-affinity, specific binding to the DNA minor groove particularly in AT-rich regions, and it also has the ability to disrupt the toxic r(CUG)expMBNL1 complex in vitro, correct MBNL1-dependent splicing defects in DM1 patient-derived cells, disperse nuclear foci without global off-target splicing effects, restore a significant portion of the aberrant splicing of Clcn1 and Serca1 transcripts in the HSALR DM1 mouse model with good tolerance, minimal cytotoxicity and no overt microbiota disturbance, thus establishing itself as a lead small-molecule RNA binder that selectively rescues DM1 molecular phenotypes with potential for further development toward DM1 therapeutics, while it is primarily explored as a lead candidate for novel antitumor or antiviral drugs, acting by interfering with DNA replication, repair, and transcription, and also holds potential as a nuclear DNA stain in biological imaging.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-dichloro-4-phenylmethoxybenzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound P7, is a compound based on the benzamidine scaffold, substituted with two chlorine atoms and a benzyloxy group on the benzene ring, with the formula C14H12Cl2N2O; it is a dichloro-substituted mono-imidamide derivative, cationic due to the imidamide group, retains the twin benzamidine cations of pentamidine but is elongated with a nine-carbon methylene linker that maximizes its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, the benzyloxy group enhances lipophilicity and steric bulk, is a pentamidine-derived small molecule optimized through 3-D shape-based similarity screening as a next-generation ligand for the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 in a TR-FRET displacement assay and partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal in DM1 cell models though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, is less selective than P1 as it binds with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CCG/3GUC or 5CAG/3GCC motifs and to the human A-site, and such compounds are researched as novel antibacterial and antiparasitic agents, with activity often attributed to the specific binding and electrostatic interaction of the cationic structure with the negatively charged DNA of the microorganism, while Compound P7 itself exemplifies how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an antimicrobial template into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics.

1-[4-(4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-3-[4-[(4S)-4-methyl-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl]phenyl]urea an analog of guanidine compounds, which are used in medicinal chemistry to target RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

RNA information: r(GCGC): This model RNA is a GC-rich hairpin with a stem composed of repeating 5'-GC/3'-CG base pairs. Its key functional role in the study was to act as a stringent control for determining the selectivity of the investigated small molecules. Due to its high thermodynamic stability, the stem was shortened to 8 base pairs to make its melting temperature comparable to the longer AU-rich hairpins. Compounds with true AU selectivity, such as molecule 2 and AU-azide, demonstrated very weak binding (Kd in the micromolar range) to r(GCGC), thereby highlighting their specificity and minimizing the potential for off-target interactions with highly abundant GC-rich regions in the transcriptome.

RNA information: The miR-183 precursor is a ~70-nt stem-loop RNA that is co-transcribed with miR-96 and miR-182 from chromosome 7q32.2 as a single pri-miRNA cluster. Unlike riboswitches, no natural small-molecule ligand has been identified that selectively binds the pre-miR-183 hairpin to regulate its own processing; instead, its biogenesis is governed by canonical Drosha/Dicer cleavage and by upstream transcriptional factors such as β-catenin/TCF, GSK3β, MYCN and PI3K/AKT . Thus, while bacterial riboswitches like B. subtilis 165ribD use direct FMN binding to maintain FMN/riboflavin homeostasis, miR-183 homeostasis is achieved through transcriptional and post-transcriptional control of its precursor rather than through ligand-induced structural switching.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 1 (Velagapudi et al., 2014), is a simplified mono-benzimidazole scaffold containing the tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a small-molecule mimic of the FMN riboswitch ligand rationally designed to bind a Drosha-site hairpin in the human pre-miR-96 precursor instead of a bacterial RNA; by replacing the ribityl-phosphate tail of FMN with a 4-(3-aminopropyl)-benzimidazole core, the compound gains chemical stability and enhanced selectivity for the miR-96 stem-loop over other miRNA precursors, can bind the precursor with high affinity in vitro, block Drosha cleavage, reduce mature miR-96 levels, thereby restoring FOXO1 tumor-suppressor expression and inducing apoptosis in breast-cancer cell lines, and in mouse xenograft models, daily oral dosing can decrease tumor growth without measurable gut-microbiota perturbation, indicating it or its analogs may serve as precision antibacterial-sparing agents for miRNA-driven cancers, while its real-world activity is designed as a Click Chemistry probe for bioconjugation and target identification, and such probes are critical for studying the pharmacodynamics and metabolism of new drug candidates by allowing tracking of the molecule and identification of its binding partners in biological systems.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil, is a synthetic small molecule with an overall U-shaped rigid skeleton, this complex molecule has a substituted pyrimidin-6-one core, linked at the 2-position to a substituted piperidine ring, which is further connected to a 2-(methylamino)pyrimidinyl-methyl group, the 4-position is substituted with a thiophenyl group, its left 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring can intercalate into the stacking plane of the RNA double helix, while the right methylamino-piperidinyl group forms weak hydrogen bonds with key guanines, such multi-heterocyclic and complex structures are characteristic of many small-molecule drugs, this compound can highly specifically bind to the aptamer pocket of the bacterial FMN riboswitch, mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN and blocking the binding of FMN to RNA, thereby relieving the negative feedback inhibition of the riboswitch on the riboflavin synthesis gene ribB, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to riboflavin depletion, and importantly, its activity can be completely reversed by exogenous riboflavin, meanwhile this compound is often designed to target specific protein kinases, by inhibiting key kinases, it may find applications in the treatment of cancer, inflammation, or autoimmune diseases.
