Small_molecule_information: 2-[(3R)-1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil-A, is a single chiral isomer, with the defined stereochemistry at the piperidine rings 3-position as (3R), and Ribocil-A is the R-configured enantiomer of ribocil, in pharmacology, the specific stereoisomer often dictates the binding affinity and efficacy towards biological targets, therefore, the activity of this compound (e.g., kinase inhibition strength) is likely to be different from its enantiomer or the racemic mixture, representing the single, potentially more potent or selective, active pharmaceutical ingredient, compared with the S-configuration, the orientation of its aromatic ring system and the spatial arrangement of the methylamino-piperidinyl group are unfavorable for intercalation into the aptamer pocket of the FMN riboswitch, resulting in a significant decrease in binding affinity, functionally, it almost loses the ability to inhibit riboswitch-mediated ribB gene expression and cannot effectively deplete intracellular riboflavin, thus essentially lacking antibacterial activity.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

RNA information: 5'CCG/3'GCC is a 1×1 CG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, with a short stem loop (5'CCG/3'GCC), no U·U mismatches, G-C/C-G pairing, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Pathogenically linked to FXTAS (via mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CCG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting proteins/ligands (e.g., diguanidinobenzoates) to modulate splicing/RAN translation. It also serves as an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-phenylhydrazinyl)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G6, is a purine compound featuring a phenylhydrazinyl group attached at the 2-position (G6 is a derivative of guanine with a phenylhydrazine group [-N-NH-PH] introduced at the C2 position). The hydrazinyl (NHNH2) structure itself is highly reactive, and the resulting phenylhydrazinyl group allows the lone pair electrons on the nitrogen atoms to participate in the purine rings conjugation system, influencing its reactivity and tautomeric equilibrium. This type of purine derivative is often synthesized as part of chemical libraries for biological screening, as the combination of the purine ring and the hydrazinyl moiety allows the molecule to interact with various biomacromolecules like proteins and nucleic acids; G6 can induce RNA to produce structural changes similar to natural guanine and has potent antibacterial activity against Bacillus subtilis, which can completely inhibit the growth of the bacterium for 24 hours in the lowest glucose medium, though this antibacterial effect is not related to the riboswitchG6 cannot inhibit the reporter gene expression controlled by the xpt-pbuX riboswitch, and the drug resistance mutation occurs in the purine efflux pump coding region rather than the riboswitch, suggesting that its antibacterial mechanism mainly depends on non-riboswitch targets. Its potential activity may include acting as an inhibitor of kinases or phosphatases, enzymes critical in cell signaling, proliferation, and apoptosis, thus exploring its use in therapeutic areas such as anti-inflammation, oncology, or neuroprotection.

Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: B. The 5 '-UTR of subtilis pbuE contains a 70-nt adenine-sensing ribose switch: its secondary structure is composed of conserved three-stem junction regions (P1-P3), where the P1 helix determines stability, and the ligand binding site is located in the core pocket; When transcription progresses to approximately the 80th to 110th nucleotide, if adenine binds, aptamer folds to prevent the formation of the downstream terminator (63-79 pairs with 83-98 bases), RNA polymerase reads through it, initiates pbuE gene expression, and generates an adenine effloression pump to resist purine toxicity. Otherwise, the terminator folds rapidly and the transcription terminates prematurely. The regulation of this switch is jointly controlled by adenine binding kinetics and the transcriptional time scale, and it can switch between kinetic and thermodynamic modes.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-6-amine, also referred to as Adenine (a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound), is one of the four fundamental nucleobases that constitute nucleic acids (DNA and RNA), structured as a purine ring with an amino group at position 6, molecular formula C5H5N5. In terms of real-world activity, Adenine is a vital building block for life; it is an essential component of genetic material, pairing with Thymine (T) in the DNA double helix and Uracil (U) in RNA via hydrogen bonds, forming the basis for storing and expressing genetic information, is the core structure of the cells energy currency ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate), ADP, and AMP, participating in all major cellular energy transfer processes, and is a constituent of several important coenzymes (e.g., NAD, FAD), involved in a wide range of redox reactions and metabolic regulation; additionally, Adenine is embedded in the core of the pbuE ribose switch with a simple 6-aminopine plane, inducing local base rearrangement and stabilizing the P1 helix, rapidly occupies RNA within the instantaneous window of transcription, thereby inhibiting terminon folding and enabling the expression of downstream efference pump genes, and serves as a natural start button for bacteria to sense and excrete excess purine and maintain intracellular homeostasis.

Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: r(CUG)109 is a pathogenic RNA motif composed of 109 CUG repeats that folds into a stable, elongated hairpin structure containing periodic 1×1 UU internal loops. It functions as a toxic RNA by specifically binding to and sequestering muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, thereby disrupting its normal splicing regulation and contributing to the molecular pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Small molecules that selectively bind r(CUG)109 can displace MBNL1, restore aberrant splicing patterns, and alleviate disease-associated phenotypes in cellular models .

RNA information: cTNT (cardiac troponin T) pre-mRNA is a splicing reporter RNA that contains an alternatively spliced exon 5. It is regulated by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, which binds upstream of exon 5 and represses its inclusion. In myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA sequesters MBNL1, leading to increased exon 5 inclusion in cTNT mRNA. Thus, cTNT splicing serves as a functional readout for MBNL1 activity and DM1-associated splicing defects in cellular models .

RNA information: r(GGGGCC)₈ is an RNA repeat sequence that forms a structured RNA motif capable of binding small molecules. It adopts both hairpin and G-quadruplex conformations and plays a pathogenic role in c9FTD/ALS by undergoing repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation and forming nuclear RNA foci. Small molecules targeting r(GGGGCC)₈ can inhibit these toxic events, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: Disodium;[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(2,6-dioxo-3H-purin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate, also referred to as Xanthosine monophosphate (XMP, or xanthylic acid) and Guanosine Monophosphate Disodium (GMP Disodium), is a non-azaaromatic purine nucleotide with the formula C10H12N5Na2O8P that exists as a disodium salt, structured with a xanthine base, a ribose sugar (the oxolan ring), and a single phosphate group linked to the riboses 5-carbon, which is composed of the guanine base (2,6-dioxo-3H-purin) linked at N9 to a D-ribose sugar that is phosphorylated at the 5 position, it lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch), and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, with its core role being as a critical intermediate in purine nucleotide biosynthesis, acting as the precursor for both guanosine monophosphate (GMP) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP) to support nucleic acid synthesis and cellular metabolism, and GMP is also a precursor for Ribonucleic Acid (RNA) and Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA), and participates in energy storage and signal transduction (e.g., in the G-protein coupled receptor system) as Guanosine Triphosphate (GTP) within the cell, in real-world application, GMP Disodium is primarily used as a food additive, specifically as a flavor enhancer, which is often combined with Inosine Monophosphate (IMP) (known as IG) to provide and intensify the savory or umami taste in foods, widely used in soups, instant noodle seasonings, snacks, and processed meats.

RNA information: (CUG)₅₀ is a toxic CUG trinucleotide repeat RNA linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it adopts a hairpin structure with multiple 1×1 UU internal loops (its key RNA Motif) and has the sequence 5'-r(CUG)₅₀-3'.Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1, causing RNA mis-splicing; it’s used as a model to screen ligands (e.g., pentamidine, EBAB) via affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) for DM1 therapy .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 24 (EBAB, Pentamidine), is an amidine linked by an alkoxy chain, where two amidine groups are connected by a relatively long pentyloxy ether chain, this molecule is an aromatic diamidine with benzamidine pharmacophore and features a 1,2-ethane/1,5-bis(4-amidinophenoxy)pentane scaffold and bisamidinium groups, its bis-amidine ether structure is highly significant in medicinal chemistry, exemplified by the well-known antiprotozoal drug Pentamidine which acts by binding to parasite DNA, it can also bind r(CUG)exps UU loops/5CUG/3GUC hairpins, displace MBNL1, correct DM1 splicing defects, act as an antibiotic adjuvant against multidrug-resistant bacteria, inhibit Class I intron self-splicing, and has low toxicity, the structural features of the molecule make it a candidate for a potential antiprotozoal or antifungal drug particularly against diseases like Pneumocystis pneumonia, and its activity is closely related to the length and flexibility of the linker chain, while also showing promise for DM1 and drug-resistant infections.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E,1Z)-2-[(2-ethyl-1,2,4a,5,5a,9a,10,10a-octahydrobenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-1,8-bis[2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-hydroxy-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1-(oxiran-2-ylidene)-8-oxooct-4-en-7-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as 1Z (the Z-isomer of the initial scaffold), is a peptidomimetic compound with a highly asymmetric structure, which demonstrates modestly improved performance compared to 1E, has affinity for the HIV-1 FSS RNA, certain selectivity over tRNA, is cell permeable and able to inhibit pseudotyped HIV infection in a dose-dependent manner (though its activity is weaker than N-methylated analogs) and lacks N-methyl substitutions thus not benefiting from the enhanced conformational bias observed in later compounds; the asymmetry of the molecule is notable in two main parts: first, it contains a fully aromatic benzo[g]quinoline group on one side and a multi-saturated octahydrobenzo[g]quinoline group on the other, leading to significant differences in aromaticity and polarity between the two ends, which may result in multi-modal interactions with its target, second, the molecular backbone includes an oxiran-2-ylidene (epoxide) structure, a high-strain, electrophilic three-membered ring, and this electrophilic group suggests its candidacy as a covalent inhibitor, capable of forming an irreversible covalent bond with nucleophilic amino acid residues (e.g., Cysteine, Lysine) in biological targets like proteases or kinases, leading to sustained activity, while its peptidomimetic portion contains a phenylalanine-polyamine derivative, offering potential interaction with the polyamine transport system or DNA, thus this molecule is likely a multifunctional anti-cancer or anti-infective lead compound with a covalent mechanism of action.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N-[4-[[4-amino-6-[3-[4-[2-[[4-amino-6-[4-[[4-[N-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butyl]carbamimidoyl]benzenecarboximidoyl]amino]butylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]ethyl]triazol-1-yl]propylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]-1-N-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 2b, is a dimeric ligand structurally analogous to 2a but featuring a slightly longer linker, it exhibits a very similar high potency to 2a for inhibiting the MBNL1-rCUGexp complex, despite its comparable in vitro efficacy, it was not selected for further cellular and animal studies due to its higher synthetic cost compared to 2a, and this is an extremely large, structurally complex oligomer or conjugate incorporating multiple functional units: a benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core, several triazine rings, multiple butane amine linkers, and a triazole ring, often introduced via click chemistry, the triazine and imidamide groups are strongly basic, making this molecule a highly charged cationic species with powerful electrostatic interaction capability, this multi-fragment complexity strongly suggests a design as a multivalent ligand, intended to bind simultaneously to multiple sites on a biological target or act as a drug delivery system, in chemical biology, structures containing triazine and imidamide are often studied as sequence-specific nucleic acid binders or potent metabolic enzyme inhibitors, with the triazole ring typically serving as a stable, bio-inert connecting unit.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)8 RNA forms an A-form structure with consecutive CU mismatches, sequestering MBNL1 protein, leading to splicing defects and DM2 disease symptoms.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-(2,4,6-triaminopyrimidin-5-yl)butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 8, is built on a highly symmetrical benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core, where the imidamide groups are strongly basic and capable of bearing a positive charge; the para-substituted core is linked via two four-carbon butane flexible chains to the terminal 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine rings, and the use of a longer butane chain compared to a propylene chain increases the overall flexibility of the molecule and modifies the effective distance between the two terminal triaminopyrimidine rings, which is critical for achieving optimal spatial alignment for binding to biological targets like nucleic acids; with multiple strongly basic, planar aromatic heterocycles, this compound is classified as a potential DNA/RNA binder, capable of disrupting nucleic acid function through multiple interactions (electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, stacking), and it also boasts the highest potency in its series, can efficiently disrupt the MBNL1-r(CCUG)8 interaction, dissolve disease foci in DM2 model cells while maintaining very low toxicity, highlighting its potential as a therapeutic agent, while such compounds are commonly screened and developed as antiparasitic drugs, particularly against parasitic organisms with distinct nucleic acid metabolism.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: tetrakis(4-methylbenzenesulfonic acid);5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is an organic salt containing a porphyrin macrocycle and toluenesulfonate counterions, which is a cationic porphyrin and a widely used pan-G-quadruplex binder that bound the r(G4C2) quadruplex with high affinity among the tested ligands and whose inability to inhibit RAN translation was particularly significant, proving that even high affinity for the quadruplex does not translate to functional activity against the pathogenic hairpin; its structure is based on the porphyrin parent skeleton: four pyrrole rings linked by methine bridges form a conjugated macrocycle, where the four meso-positions of the macrocycle are substituted with 1-methylpyridinium-4-yl groups, and in terms of biological function, porphyrin compounds, due to their unique structure and photosensitivity, are often used in photodynamic therapy, bioimaging probes, and as DNA-binding agents (e.g., TMPyP).

Small_molecule_information: 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide, also referred to as BMVC/BMVC2/BMVC4, is a dicationic compound built on a carbazole scaffold, where two 1-methylpyridinium groups are attached at positions 3 and 6 via trans-ethenyl linkers, with the formula C28H27I2N3; as a series of carbazole derivatives and well-known fluorescent probes with high affinity for DNA/RNA G-quadruplex structures, it served as a representative G-quadruplex ligand in a related study, and despite binding strongly to the G-quadruplex form of r(G4C2)exp in vitro, it was a poor inhibitor of RAN translation in cellular models, while its carbazole core provides optoelectronic properties, the long conjugated chain and dicationic nature contribute to its strong fluorescence and nucleobase affinity, it is typically employed as a high-performance fluorescent dye in biological systems, and biologically, its ability to electrostatically bind to and potentially intercalate with biomacromolecules suggests potential anticancer and antimicrobial activities by disrupting cell membrane function or DNA replication.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide, also referred to as NaphDH5 which is similar to NaphDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and exhibited good G4 stabilization but only moderate FID affinity and no biological effect, with poor drug-likeness, possesses a unique, highly strained 2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene core, which is a fused system of an eight-membered ring and a four-membered ring, along with a carbohydrazide functionality. The molecule contains a quaternized pyridinium ring, specifically the pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl group, linked via a benzoyl moiety, with bromide as the counterion. The combination of this complex, rigid bicyclic system, the carbohydrazide group, and the cationic pyridinium suggests that 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide may be explored as a non-classical -lactam analog antibiotic or as an active site inhibitor for bacterial enzymes, leveraging its rigid structure and cationic charge for target binding. Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)phenyl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as the benzimidazole-based RNA-binding substance, is a probe that is based on a mono-benzimidazole scaffold (instead of bis-benzimidazole) but still includes the tert-butyl and azide groups, and its core structure is benzimidazole which is known to have a binding preference for RNA, it also features a methylphenylpiperazine moiety that is crucial for its RNA-binding capability, and the azide group tag facilitates immobilization onto microarrays via click chemistry; mono-benzimidazoles are often precursors for antiviral, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory drugs, the presence of the azide group dictates its real-world activity as a Click Chemistry probe, it is designed to identify the biological targets of this specific mono-benzimidazole derivative through subsequent labeling and target characterization; in terms of binding properties, this compound does not bind to DNA but exclusively binds to specific RNA bulge structures, its binding to target RNAs has moderate affinity, and it specifically inhibits the processing of miR-17, -18a, and -20a by Dicer thereby suppressing the maturation of these oncogenic miRNAs; regarding its biological function, in cells it inhibits the production of miR-18a which in turn leads to the derepression of the STK4 protein and induces apoptosis in prostate cancer cells, additionally its specific binding to pre-miR-18a in cells has been validated using Chem-CLIP (Chemical Cross-Linking and Isolation by Pull-down) technology.

Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4c-6, is a large, planar quaternary ammonium salt based on the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline core, which demonstrated binding affinity for the NRQ, exhibited variable anti-proliferative activity with efficacy in A375 cells but no effect in HeLa cells at relevant concentrations; quaternary salts are often potent DNA intercalators and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents due to their positive charge and ability to disrupt cell membranes, and this structure is typically investigated for its strong anticancer activity.

Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PymDH4, presented as the dibromide salt, is a symmetric bis-cationic polymethine dye structure that contains a central pyrimidine core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and showed very poor G4 binding. It features a central pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core symmetrically linked via Schiff base connections to two permanently charged 1-ethylquinolin-1-ium cations. This molecule is a prime example of a nucleic acid fluorescent intercalator. Its bis-cationic nature and extended planar conjugation allow for strong binding to DNA or RNA through a combination of electrostatic attraction and stacking, typically exhibiting fluorescence enhancement upon binding. In reality, 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide is used as a nuclear stain, a DNA quantitation reagent, or a potential anticancer agent designed to disrupt nucleic acid function.

RNA information: miR-1587 is a G-rich microRNA that forms a stable parallel G-quadruplex structure, confirmed by biophysical methods. It directly targets and downregulates TAGLN, an important tumor suppressor and marker of smooth muscle differentiation, at both mRNA and protein levels. Its function is structurally modulated: KCl-induced G-quadruplex formation inhibits its activity by blocking seed region access. Ligands further regulate its function—pseudopalmatine stabilizes the quadruplex and enhances suppression, while TMPyP4 disrupts the structure and restores miRNA-mediated repression.
Small_molecule_information: 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is a tetracationic porphyrin-like compound where the dihydroporphyrin (chlorin) core is substituted at the four meso positions (5, 10, 15, 20) with four 1-methylpyridinium groups, with the formula C44H38N8 (excluding counterions); this compound is also a cationic porphyrin known as a G-quadruplex ligand that acts as a destabilizer of the miR-1587 G-quadruplex, which can disrupt and prevent the formation of the stable parallel G-quadruplex structure induced by potassium ions (as evidenced by a concentration-dependent decrease in the characteristic CD spectral signal of the quadruplex), and by binding to and unfolding the secondary structure, it frees the seed region of miR-1587 to make it available for interaction with its complementary target mRNA sequence, thereby enhancing the functional activity of miR-1587, leading to increased binding to the TAGLN 3UTR and stronger repression of the reporter gene, while its effect is negligible on a mutated target plasmid, confirming that its function is specifically tied to enabling miRNA-mRNA binding rather than direct interference with the translation machinery; in addition, these tetracationic porphyrin derivatives are prominent photosensitizers, and the chlorin core enables highly efficient absorption of visible light and subsequent generation of reactive oxygen species (like singlet oxygen, 1O2), with its real-world application lying in Photodynamic Therapy (PDT), where it is designed to selectively accumulate in tumor tissues and, upon light activation, specifically kill cancer cells through oxidative stress.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine, also referred to as a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an N1,N1-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine side chain at the 11-position, has the N,N-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine side chain, a short-chain dimethyl tertiary amine derivative; this compound exhibited moderate binding affinity to the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex (NRQ) and showed potent anti-proliferative activity across all tested cancer cell lines including notable activity in A375 melanoma cells, and the strong basicity of the tertiary amine terminus results in a positively charged species at physiological pH, promoting strong electrostatic interactions and accumulation in acidic organelles, which makes it a potent DNA binding agent and a lead compound for lysosome-targeting anticancer drugs.

RNA information: Located at positions 36–55 in the KRAS 5′-UTR. It forms a parallel RG4 with Tm = 64°C and ΔG = –3.7 kcal/mol. It binds strongly to BG4 antibody and is highly stabilized by ligands 2a and 2b, showing significant translational repression in luciferase assays.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione, also referred to as a furan-fused anthraquinone derivative with bis(2-aminoethylamino) side chains, is a compound based on a Naphthoquinone core fused with a Benzofuran ring, resulting in a Naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione structure, substituted with two 2-aminoethylamino groups at positions C-4 and C-11. Anthraquinone/Naphthoquinone compounds are a well-known class of DNA topoisomerase inhibitors and DNA intercalators, forming the basis of many chemotherapy drugs (e.g., Mitoxantrone). Additionally, these quinone structures readily undergo redox cycling, generating Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) that cause DNA damage and cell apoptosis, and this compound also exhibits high cellular uptake, strong stabilization of KRAS RG4s and specific binding affinity, which enables it to effectively inhibit translation and induce apoptosis in pancreatic cancer cells, meaning it is therefore primarily researched as a novel anti-tumor agent with a mechanism involving dual DNA intercalation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-2-[2-(3,4,5-trimethoxyphenyl)ethenyl]quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 11, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative and a Cyanine Dye analog (monocationic), which has a 3,4,5-trimethoxystyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring, no substitution at the 4-position, and its phenyl ring substituent is the well-known 3,4,5-trimethoxyphenyl group; the trimethoxyphenyl scaffold (also known as the combretastatin scaffold) is present in many natural products with anti-microtubule activity (such as colchicine), and this compound combines the fluorescent and intercalating properties of the cyanine dye with the anti-tumor potential of the trimethoxyphenyl group, additionally it has certain affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence) as shown by SPR assay, was not tested in the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its effect on ADAM10 translation is unknown, and exhibits cytotoxicity; in practical applications, this dual-functional structure is often explored as a novel anticancer lead compound that can both track cells via fluorescence and exert cytotoxic effects by interfering with microtubules or acting as an intercalating agent.

Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-[(E)-2-[1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium-2-yl]ethenyl]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 24, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative linking carbazole and quinoline quaternary ammonium salt via a vinyl group, which has a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the quinoline 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position; its large -conjugated system imparts excellent fluorescence, making it useful as a cationic stain, biological probe, or for OLED materials, and it has been confirmed by SPR and MST to have high affinity for A10-RNA-Wt, can enhance ADAM10 translation in a concentration-dependent manner, boost sAPP secretion, reduce A, and exhibits certain cytotoxicity towards HeLa cells.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[4-[2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]phenyl]morpholine, also referred to as Compound 14, is a green-yellow cationic fluorescent dye linking 1-methylquinolin-1-ium with a morpholine-substituted phenyl group, which is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 4-morpholinostyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and no substitution at the 4-position; the morpholine group acts as an electron donor, creating a powerful push-pull electronic system that yields high fluorescence quantum yield and environmental sensitivity, and the dyes lipophilicity allows for good cell membrane permeability, and it is used as a fluorescent probe for cellular imaging, potentially targeting specific organelles like mitochondria, while SPR assay shows it has weak affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence), dual-luciferase reporter assay reveals it inhibits ADAM10 translation, and it has certain cytotoxicity against HeLa and HEK-APP cells.
